name: List Scheduling
summary: Workhorse instruction-scheduling algorithm: build dependency DAG, topologically order, at each cycle pick the 'best' ready instruction via heuristic. Greedy and provably non-optimal but near-linear time; every production backend uses a variant. Pre-RA vs post-RA scheduling are distinct trade-offs.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
After instruction selection, the backend must order instructions for optimal pipeline use. **List scheduling** (Gibbons & Muchnick, Adams & Gibbons 1976 era) is the workhorse algorithm: build a dependency DAG, topologically order, and at each cycle pick the "best" ready instruction using a heuristic — critical path length, register pressure, resource availability.

List scheduling is a greedy heuristic — provably not optimal, but runs in near-linear time and produces good schedules in practice. Every production backend (LLVM `MachineScheduler`, GCC `haifa-sched`) uses a variant with target-specific heuristics tuned per-microarchitecture (Skylake vs Zen vs Graviton).

sections.title: Algorithm
sections.content:
Build the dependency DAG: each instruction is a node; edges represent dependences (data, anti, output, resource). For each node, compute the *critical path* — the longest path from this node to the end of the block — typically the dominant priority heuristic.

Maintain a *ready set* of instructions whose dependences are all satisfied. At each cycle, pick the highest-priority ready instruction (longest critical path, tie-broken by other heuristics like "reduces register pressure" or "keeps functional unit busy"), schedule it, and update the ready set.

The heuristic choice dominates code quality. Common heuristics include: longest-path-first, register-pressure-aware (defer instructions that would increase live values), resource-aware (prefer instructions that fill unused functional units), and microarchitecture-specific tuning (Intel scheduling differs from AMD differs from ARM).

sections.title: Pre-RA vs post-RA scheduling
sections.content:
The structural trade-off: **pre-RA scheduling** maximises ILP but can raise register pressure, causing spills; **post-RA scheduling** respects physical registers but has less freedom. Most backends do *both* — a pre-RA pass for ILP, then a post-RA pass to clean up after allocation.

Pre-RA scheduling sees virtual registers and unbounded reorder freedom but has no clue how the allocator will assign physical registers. Aggressive pre-RA scheduling can spread live ranges so wide that the allocator runs out of registers and spills. Pre-RA schedulers therefore include register-pressure heuristics that bound how aggressively they reorder.

Post-RA scheduling sees physical registers, so dependences include hardware-imposed pseudo-dependences (the same register can't hold two values simultaneously). The freedom is limited but the schedule is final; this is where target-specific microarchitectural tuning lives.
